claims.claim_text: SSH encrypts the session, but saying "Nobody can listen in on your conversation" overstates the guarantee.
claims.span_hint: section: SSH: Your Secure Tunnel to Remote Kitchens
claims.anchor_text: The "secure" part is important: everything you send over SSH is encrypted. Nobody can listen in on your conversation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The "secure" part is important: SSH encrypts the session in transit, so people on the network cannot read the commands and output.
claims.rationale: RFC 4251 supports confidentiality and integrity over an insecure network, but the original wording is too absolute beyond the in-transit threat model.

claims.claim_text: Passwords and SSH keys are common SSH authentication methods, but they are not the only ones.
claims.anchor_text: There are two ways to prove your identity to a remote server:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Two common ways to prove your identity to a remote server are:
claims.rationale: OpenSSH and the SSH specs support additional authentication methods, so the exclusive phrasing is inaccurate.

claims.claim_text: Running `ssh-keygen -t ed25519` and accepting the default save location typically creates `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` and `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`.
claims.rationale: The GitHub docs source shows the default `id_ALGORITHM` save path and subsequent `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` usage for Ed25519 keys.

claims.claim_text: RFC 4251 defines SSH as a protocol for secure remote login and other secure network services over an insecure network.
claims.rationale: This is stated directly in RFC 4251.

claims.claim_text: SSH was designed as a replacement for older remote-login tools such as Telnet.
claims.rationale: The overview history on Secure Shell explicitly describes SSH as a replacement for Telnet and related insecure remote-shell tools.

claims.claim_text: Port 22 is the default assigned port for SSH.
claims.rationale: The Secure Shell overview records port 22 as the protocol's assigned default port.

claims.claim_text: OpenSSH is software for remote login, command execution, and related SSH operations.
claims.rationale: The OpenSSH upstream README directly describes the project as a complete SSH implementation for secure remote login and command execution.

claims.claim_text: On Ubuntu systems where the SSH server component is missing, installing `openssh-server` is the documented way to enable SSH server functionality.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On Exercise: SSH to Localhost / On Linux
claims.rationale: Ubuntu's primary documentation directly supports the package-install guidance, but the host is outside the current allowlist.

further_reading.title: RFC 4251: The Secure Shell (SSH) Protocol Architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the core standards document behind SSH's security model and protocol definition.

further_reading.title: Portable OpenSSH
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the upstream OpenSSH project that most learners will encounter on Unix-like systems.

further_reading.title: GitHub Docs Source: Generating a New SSH Key
further_reading.why_relevant: It gives a practical, beginner-friendly walkthrough of creating and using SSH keys.

notes: Per schema, no claim entries were created for analogies, quiz prompts, exercise steps, code blocks, or the comparison table; the Kubernetes cluster-size example was treated as beginner-oriented illustrative framing rather than a citation-bearing hard claim.
